planting.note: Sow in early spring or late summer; cold improves flavor.

directions: Sow this blend of curly, flat-leaf, and oak-leaf kales thickly in a band for baby-leaf harvests, or thin to 6 inches for full-size plants. Direct-seed 1/4 inch deep as soon as soil can be worked in spring, and again in late summer for a fall crop. Cut outer leaves at 3-4 inches for tender salad greens, harvesting cut-and-come-again over several weeks. Cold weather and light frost sweeten the leaves, so this mix carries well into winter in mild zones.

planting.note: Start indoors, transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Ace 55-VF is a determinate heirloom bearing round, low-acid red fruits of 5-6 inches with thick, juicy flesh, and it carries resistance to verticillium and fusarium wilt. Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before your last frost, then harden off and transplant once nights stay above 50F. Give plants a sturdy cage even though the compact vines are self-limiting, and mulch to keep soil moisture even and prevent blossom-end rot. Because it sets much of its crop at once, this variety is well suited to canning and batch preserving.

planting.note: Long-day onion; start early indoors for northern gardens.

directions: Ailsa Craig is a long-day heirloom producing very large, globe-shaped golden bulbs with a mild, sweet flavor, best grown in northern latitudes. Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost and set out seedlings as soon as the soil is workable, spacing 4-6 inches apart. Keep the bed weed-free and evenly watered through bulbing, then taper off water as tops begin to fall. These sweet onions store only a few months, so use the largest bulbs first.

planting.note: Succession sow spring through fall; heat-tolerant and slow to bolt.

directions: This adaptable heirloom lettuce forms crisp, mild green heads and resists bolting better than most, letting you harvest across spring, summer, and fall. Sow seed shallowly, barely covering it, since light aids germination, and thin plants to 8-12 inches for full heads. Succession-sow every two to three weeks for a continuous supply, and provide afternoon shade during hot spells to keep leaves sweet. It also performs well in containers and hydroponic setups.

planting.note: Start indoors early; seeds benefit from cold stratification.

directions: Alpine Regina is an everbearing woodland strawberry that produces small, intensely aromatic berries all season on tidy, runnerless clumps. Surface-sow the tiny seeds indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost, pressing them onto moist mix without covering, since light aids germination; a few weeks of cold stratification improves the sprouting rate. Transplant seedlings 8-12 inches apart in rich, well-drained soil with morning sun and some afternoon shade. Plants make an excellent edible border or container crop and often fruit the first year.

planting.note: Start indoors early; extremely cold-hardy for fall and winter harvest.

directions: American Flag is a cold-hardy heirloom leek with long white shanks, blue-green leaves, and a mild, sweet onion flavor that holds in the garden through fall and into winter. Start seed indoors 10-12 weeks before the last frost, then transplant into holes 6 inches deep to blanch the stems white. Hill soil up around the shanks as they grow to lengthen the tender white portion. Harvest as needed once shanks reach an inch across; the plants tolerate hard frost and can be dug even after snow.

planting.note: Start indoors, transplant after all frost danger.

directions: Amish Paste is a vigorous indeterminate heirloom yielding large, plum-to-oxheart-shaped fruits of 8-12 ounces with dense, richly flavored flesh ideal for sauce and canning. Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights are reliably warm. Provide tall stakes or a strong trellis and prune suckers to improve air flow, since the sprawling vines produce all season. Keep watering even and mulch well to reduce cracking and blossom-end rot on the heavy fruit.

planting.note: Start indoors with warmth; transplant well after frost.

directions: Anaheim, also called New Mexico chile, is a mild heirloom pepper producing long, tapered fruits with a smoky flavor prized for roasting, stuffing, and grilling. Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost using bottom heat, since the seeds need warm soil to sprout. Transplant only after nights stay above 55F, spacing plants about 18-24 inches apart in full sun. Harvest green for chiles rellenos or leave fruit to ripen red for a sweeter, deeper flavor.

planting.note: Start indoors early; transplant crowns to a permanent bed.

directions: Argenteuil is an award-winning French heirloom asparagus with tender, sweet purple-and-green spears tipped in pink, valued for early, heavy yields. Start seed indoors 10-12 weeks before the last frost, soaking seed first to speed the slow germination, then transplant seedlings to a deeply prepared permanent bed. Wait until the third spring to begin cutting spears so the crowns can build strong roots, and let ferns grow all summer to feed the plants. A well-tended bed remains productive for 15 to 20 years.

planting.note: Direct sow after soil warms; heat-loving vine.

directions: Armenian Pale Green is actually a heat-tolerant melon grown like a cucumber, producing long, ribbed, pale-skinned fruits that stay crisp and mild without bitterness. Direct-sow after the soil warms to at least 70F, planting seeds about an inch deep, and thin or transplant to 12-18 inches apart. Grow on a trellis to keep the long fruits straight and clean, harvesting at 12-18 inches while the skin is still tender. Because the thin skin needs no peeling, these are excellent for fresh salads and quick pickles.

planting.note: Succession sow spring and fall; bolts quickly in heat.

directions: Arugula is a fast-growing, cool-season green with a bold, peppery bite that is delicious in salads, on pizza, and lightly wilted. Direct-sow shallowly every two to three weeks from early spring through fall for a steady supply of tender leaves. Harvest young outer leaves cut-and-come-again, and cut the whole plant before hot weather triggers bolting and a sharper flavor. In warm regions grow it in partial shade or indoors to extend the harvest window.

planting.note: Direct sow after soil is thoroughly warm; loves heat.

directions: This heat-loving Asian cowpea is a vigorous pole bean that produces slender pods up to a meter long, best picked young at 12-18 inches for tender eating. Direct-sow after the soil has thoroughly warmed to 70F or more, planting an inch deep at the base of a tall, sturdy trellis. The climbing vines thrive in hot, humid weather where common beans struggle and keep producing with regular harvest. Pick pods frequently while still smooth and firm to encourage continued set.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; very long season, start indoors in short-season areas.

directions: Atlantic Giant is the classic record-breaking pumpkin, capable of producing fruits weighing hundreds of pounds under ideal care. Sow seed after all frost danger in deeply amended, compost-rich soil, giving each plant sprawling room of 10 feet or more. For the largest fruit, allow only one or two pumpkins per vine and remove the rest, then feed and water generously all season. Support the growing fruit on sand or a board to prevent rot, and protect the tender vines from any late frost.

planting.note: Start indoors, transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Aunt Ruby's German Green is an indeterminate heirloom beefsteak that ripens to a green-and-amber blush and offers a sweet, slightly spicy, well-balanced flavor. Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once the weather is settled and warm. Cage or stake the tall vines and prune suckers for airflow, since the large fruits can reach a pound each. Judge ripeness by a slight softening and yellow-green color rather than by red, harvesting before the fruit becomes overly soft.

planting.note: Start indoors, transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Beefsteak is the classic large-fruited slicing tomato, an indeterminate heirloom producing meaty red fruits around four inches across that are perfect for sandwiches and burgers. Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and set out sturdy transplants after nights warm past 50F, burying the stem deeply to root along its length. Support the tall vines with strong cages or stakes and prune lower suckers to improve air circulation. Keep soil evenly moist and mulched to avoid cracking and blossom-end rot on the heavy fruit.

planting.note: Direct sow after soil warms; can start indoors 3 weeks early.

directions: Beit Alpha is a burpless, thin-skinned heirloom cucumber that is parthenocarpic, setting smooth, mild fruit without pollination and free of bitterness. Direct-sow after the soil warms to 70F, or start indoors three weeks early, and grow up a trellis to keep fruit straight and clean. Harvest at 4-6 inches while the skin is deep green and tender, picking often to keep the vines producing. Because no peeling is needed, these cucumbers are ideal for fresh salads and snacking.

planting.note: Start indoors with warmth; transplant well after frost.

directions: Big Red is a sweet heirloom bell pepper with thick walls and large, blocky three-to-four-lobed fruits that ripen from green to deep red and grow sweeter as they color. Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost with bottom heat, and transplant only after nights stay above 55F. Space plants 18-24 inches apart in full sun and stake taller plants to keep the heavy fruit off the ground. Pick green for a crisp, mild flavor or leave on the plant to ripen fully red for maximum sweetness.

planting.note: Start indoors; needs a long, hot growing season.

directions: Bitter melon, also called bitter gourd or karela, is a tropical climbing vine grown for its warty green fruit widely used in Asian cooking. Soak the hard seeds overnight and start indoors 4-6 weeks before the last frost, since the plant demands a long, hot season. Transplant to full sun after the soil is warm and train the vigorous vines up a strong trellis. Harvest fruit young and green for the mildest flavor, picking often since the gourds turn orange and split when overripe.

planting.note: Start indoors with warmth; transplant well after frost.

directions: Black Beauty is the classic heirloom eggplant, producing large, glossy deep-purple fruits with tender, mild flesh perfect for grilling and roasting. Start seed indoors 8-10 weeks before the last frost with warm soil, and transplant only after nights reliably stay above 55F. Space plants 18-24 inches apart in full sun and stake them to support the weight of the fruit. Harvest while the skin is firm and glossy, cutting rather than pulling; picking regularly keeps the plants productive.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Black Beauty is a dependable, prolific heirloom zucchini bearing glossy dark-green fruit on compact, bushy plants. Direct-sow two or three seeds per hill after the soil warms to 70F, then thin to the strongest seedling, spacing hills two to three feet apart. Harvest zucchini young at 6-8 inches for the best texture, and pick every day or two at peak season to keep the plant producing. Water evenly at the base and mulch to reduce disease and keep fruit clean.

planting.note: Start indoors, transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Black Cherry is an indeterminate heirloom producing clusters of round, dark mahogany cherry tomatoes with a sweet, rich, and complex flavor. Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and transplant sturdy seedlings once nights stay above 50F, setting them deep. Give the vigorous vines a tall cage or trellis and prune suckers for airflow, since they can top six feet. Harvest when fruit turns deep purple-brown and gives slightly to the touch; frequent picking keeps the clusters coming all season.

planting.note: Sow spring and late summer; very cold-hardy.

directions: Black Knight tatsoi is a heirloom Asian green forming tidy rosettes of dark, spoon-shaped leaves with a sweet, mild mustard flavor. Direct-sow shallowly in early spring and again in late summer, thinning plants to 6-10 inches for full rosettes or leaving them close for baby greens. Harvest outer leaves cut-and-come-again, or cut the whole rosette; the plant tolerates hard frost and sweetens in cold weather. It grows quickly and does well in containers and cold frames for winter harvests.

planting.note: Start indoors, transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Black Krim is a Crimean heirloom beefsteak bearing large, dark purple-brown fruits with a rich, full-bodied, faintly smoky and salty flavor. Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights are reliably warm. Support the indeterminate vines with a strong cage or stake and prune suckers for air movement to reduce cracking in the shoulders. Harvest when the fruit develops its dark color and gives slightly; the color deepens in hot weather and sun.

planting.note: Succession sow spring and fall; light aids germination.

directions: Black Seeded Simpson is a fast, reliable heirloom loose-leaf lettuce with tender, light-green ruffled leaves and a mild, crisp flavor. Direct-sow shallowly, barely covering the seed since light aids germination, and thin plants to 6-10 inches apart. Harvest outer leaves cut-and-come-again for a prolonged supply, and succession-sow every two weeks from early spring through fall. Provide afternoon shade in hot weather to delay bolting; it also grows well in containers and hydroponics.

planting.note: Direct sow after soil is warm; thrives in heat.

directions: Black-eyed peas are a heat-tolerant cowpea grown for their nutritious seeds and their ability to fix nitrogen and improve soil. Direct-sow after the soil warms to 70F, planting an inch deep and thinning to 3-6 inches apart, since the plants dislike transplanting. They tolerate heat and drought well, so water moderately and avoid overly rich soil, which favors foliage over pods. Harvest young pods for fresh snap peas or leave them to dry on the plant for shelling and storage.

planting.note: Sow early spring and fall; germinates in cool soil, bolts in heat.

directions: Bloomsdale is a cold-hardy heirloom spinach with heavily crinkled, deep-green leaves and a sweet, mild flavor that stands up well in cool weather. Direct-sow in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked and again in late summer, since seed germinates poorly in warm soil. Thin plants to 4-6 inches apart and harvest outer leaves cut-and-come-again, or cut whole plants before hot weather triggers bolting. In mild zones a fall planting under cover can overwinter for an early spring harvest.
